county_metadata.record_losses: Union Army burned Mora during Civil War (1862); some early records lost.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1860 from Taos County (some sources say from San Miguel). Mora was part of the Mora Land Grant. Colfax and Harding counties carved out. Union forces burned the town during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mora County has deep Spanish colonial roots — the Mora Valley was settled by Hispanic families who received the Mora Land Grant in the 1830s. The town of Mora was burned by Union forces during the Civil War (1862), causing some early record losses, though church records survived. Catholic parish records from St. Gertrude's in Mora and surrounding missions are essential and available on FamilySearch. The Mora Land Grant records at SRCA document original grantee families. County Clerk in Mora has reconstructed deed records from the 1860s. The county has a strong Penitente (Brotherhood of Our Father Jesus) tradition — Brotherhood records are largely private but referenced in parish records. Fort Union National Monument (nearby) has military records at NARA. NM Highlands University in Las Vegas holds regional manuscripts.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Some early records reconstructed after Civil War burning.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Minor gaps from 1862 fire; mostly reconstructed

source_profiles.source_name: St. Gertrude's & Mora Valley Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Mora, Guadalupita, Cleveland, and surrounding villages. Key for Hispanic families in the Mora Valley.
Mexican-era land grant to settlers of the Mora Valley. Original petitions, surveys, and Court of Private Land Claims adjudication.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
Federal census 1860-1950. Pre-1860 area under Taos County.
Post returns, personnel records for Fort Union, the largest military installation in the Southwest during the territorial period.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1899 from Dona Ana, Lincoln, and Socorro counties. Named for Miguel A. Otero, NM territorial governor. Alamogordo is county seat. Contains Mescalero Apache Reservation, White Sands, and Holloman AFB.
county_metadata.research_tips: Otero County spans from the Tularosa Basin to the Sacramento Mountains and includes both White Sands Missile Range (Trinity Site) and the Mescalero Apache Reservation. Pre-1899 records are in Dona Ana (Las Cruces), Lincoln (Carrizozo), and Socorro counties. County Clerk in Alamogordo has deeds from 1899. The Tularosa Basin Historical Society has local materials. Holloman Air Force Base records document military families from WWII forward. Mescalero Apache tribal enrollment and BIA records at NARA Denver are essential for Apache families. The Alamogordo-Otero County area saw railroad and lumber company development — El Paso & Northeastern Railway records and Sacramento Mountains lumber company records trace settler families. Cloudcroft developed as a resort; hotel and business records supplement genealogy.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Railroad and lumber company transfers prominent.
Marriage licenses from 1899.
Census rolls, allotment records, agency correspondence for Mescalero Apache.
Military personnel records from Holloman AFB and White Sands Proving Ground.
Federal census from 1900.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1903 from Guadalupe and Union counties. Named for Matthew Quay, US Senator from Pennsylvania. Tucumcari is county seat — Route 66 town. Curry County carved out 1909.
county_metadata.research_tips: Quay County sits on the eastern NM plains where the Rock Island Railroad brought homesteaders in the early 1900s. Tucumcari became a classic Route 66 town. Pre-1903 records are in Guadalupe (Santa Rosa) and Union (Clayton) counties. County Clerk in Tucumcari has deeds from 1903. The Tucumcari Historical Museum has local family files. Many settlers came from Texas and Oklahoma during the homesteading boom of 1900-1920. Protestant church records (Baptist, Methodist) supplement civil records. BLM GLO records for homestead patents are essential — many claims were abandoned during droughts. The Quay County Genealogical Society has compiled indices. Curry County was carved from Quay in 1909.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Homesteading-era land patents and transfers.
Marriage licenses from 1903.
Wills and estates from formation.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM GLO — Quay County Homestead Patents
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal homestead patents for eastern NM plains.
Federal census from 1910.

county_metadata.record_losses: Some courthouse records damaged in 1967 Tierra Amarilla raid by Reies Lopez Tijerina's land grant movement.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Spanish colonial settlement from 1600s. Contains Ohkay Owingeh (San Juan Pueblo), Santa Clara Pueblo, and part of Jicarilla Apache Reservation. Tierra Amarilla land grant dispute epicenter.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rio Arriba County is among the most historically significant counties in the US for Spanish colonial genealogy. Ohkay Owingeh (formerly San Juan Pueblo) was the site of the first Spanish capital of New Mexico under Juan de Onate in 1598. Catholic parish records from the Santa Cruz de la Canada church (founded 1695) and other missions are on FamilySearch and date to the late 1600s. The Tierra Amarilla Land Grant and other community grants are central to the county's identity — the 1967 courthouse raid by Reies Lopez Tijerina caused some record damage. County Clerk in Tierra Amarilla has deeds from 1852. SANM I & II at SRCA contain colonial records for Rio Arriba families. Jicarilla Apache Reservation records at NARA Denver cover the western portion. The Martinez Hacienda in Taos and Abiquiu genizaro community records provide unique research opportunities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Tierra Amarilla

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial organization. Land grant conveyances predate county system.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Minor damage from 1967 courthouse incident; largely intact

source_profiles.source_name: Santa Cruz de la Canada & Rio Arriba Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers from some of NM's oldest missions. Santa Cruz de la Canada, Abiquiu, Chimayo, and surrounding parishes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilmed by FamilySearch; among oldest records in American West

source_profiles.source_name: Tierra Amarilla & Rio Arriba Land Grants
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Multiple Spanish and Mexican land grants including Tierra Amarilla, Ojo Caliente, Plaza Blanca, and others. Court of Private Land Claims files.

source_profiles.source_name: Ohkay Owingeh & Santa Clara Pueblo Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Northern Pueblos Agency
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA census rolls and agency records for San Juan (Ohkay Owingeh) and Santa Clara Pueblos.
Federal census 1850-1950. Spanish colonial padrones at SRCA for earlier periods.

source_profiles.source_name: Rio Arriba County Territorial Court Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: District court records including land grant adjudication, criminal cases, and probate matters.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1903 from Chaves and Guadalupe counties. Named for Theodore Roosevelt. Portales is county seat — eastern NM agricultural center. Eastern NM University located here. Curry County carved out 1909.
county_metadata.research_tips: Roosevelt County on NM's eastern plains was settled during the homesteading boom of the early 1900s, drawing families primarily from Texas, Oklahoma, and Arkansas. County Clerk in Portales has deeds from 1903; pre-1903 records in Chaves (Roswell) and Guadalupe (Santa Rosa). Eastern New Mexico University in Portales holds regional archives including the Blackwater Draw archaeological site records. The Roosevelt County Museum has local history materials. Dry farming and ranching dominate — check BLM GLO for homestead patents and NM Livestock Board for brand records. Protestant churches (Baptist, Church of Christ, Methodist) are primary religious sources. The county was heavily impacted by Dust Bowl conditions in the 1930s; many families left, making pre-1940 records most genealogically productive.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Homesteading-era patents and transfers.
Marriage licenses from 1903.
Wills and estates from formation.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM GLO — Roosevelt County Homestead Patents
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal homestead patents for eastern NM.
Federal census from 1910.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1887 from Rio Arriba County. Named for the San Juan River. Aztec is county seat; Farmington is largest city. Eastern portion of Navajo Nation and Jicarilla Apache Reservation overlap. Oil and gas industry center.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Juan County in NW NM is split between Navajo Nation lands and non-tribal areas around Farmington. The Navajo Nation Vital Records office in Window Rock, AZ, and BIA records at NARA Denver are essential for Navajo families. Pre-1887 records are in Rio Arriba County (Tierra Amarilla). County Clerk in Aztec has deeds from 1887. The Farmington Museum and San Juan County Historical Society have local materials. Oil and gas industry records from the 1920s onward trace many worker families. Aztec Ruins National Monument documents pre-contact Ancestral Puebloan occupation. Mormon settlers in Kirtland and Fruitland areas left LDS ward records. The Center for Southwest Research at UNM holds manuscript collections related to San Juan County. Trading post records document Navajo-trader relationships.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Much land is Navajo trust not in county system.

source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / Navajo Nation Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Census rolls, agency correspondence, and school records for eastern Navajo Nation in San Juan County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: At NARA Denver and Window Rock

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1887.
Wills, estates from territorial period.
Federal census from 1890 (1890 census lost). Navajo enumerated on separate Indian schedules.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Las Vegas (NM) is county seat — important Camino Real and Santa Fe Trail stop. Guadalupe, Quay, and part of Harding carved out. NM Highlands University located here.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Miguel County's seat of Las Vegas, NM, was one of the most important towns on the Santa Fe Trail and Camino Real, making it a crossroads of Hispanic, Anglo-American, and Jewish commercial families. Catholic parish records from Our Lady of Sorrows in Las Vegas date to the 1830s and are on FamilySearch. County Clerk in Las Vegas has deeds from 1852. NM Highlands University holds the Arrott Collection and Donnelly Library special collections with regional manuscripts. The Las Vegas land grant (Vegas Grandes) records at SRCA document original settler families. The Rough Riders Memorial Collection documents NM volunteers in the Spanish-American War. SANM records at SRCA cover colonial families. Notorious outlaw and lawman history (Doc Holliday, Vicente Silva) generated extensive court records at SRCA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Las Vegas

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from territorial organization. Las Vegas Grant conveyances extensive.

source_profiles.source_name: Our Lady of Sorrows & Las Vegas Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Las Vegas and surrounding communities. Key for tracing Hispanic families of the eastern NM settlements.

source_profiles.source_name: Las Vegas Grant & San Miguel Land Grants
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Las Vegas Grandes community grant and individual grants in the area. Court of Private Land Claims files.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
Federal census 1850-1950. Colonial padrones at SRCA for earlier periods.

source_profiles.source_name: Las Vegas Optic & La Voz del Pueblo
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Both English and Spanish language newspapers from Las Vegas. Legal notices, land grant news, obituaries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1903 from Bernalillo County. Named for the Sandoval family, prominent NM political dynasty. Contains Zia, Santa Ana, Jemez, San Felipe, Santo Domingo (Kewa), and Cochiti Pueblos, plus part of Jicarilla Apache Reservation. Los Alamos carved out 1949.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sandoval County contains six Pueblos (Zia, Santa Ana, Jemez, San Felipe, Kewa/Santo Domingo, Cochiti) each with distinct records. Catholic mission records for these Pueblos — especially Jemez (San Diego de Jemez, 1621) and Cochiti — are among the oldest in the Western Hemisphere and available on FamilySearch. Pre-1903 records are in Bernalillo County (Albuquerque). County Clerk in Bernalillo (town, not county) has deeds from 1903. BIA Northern and Southern Pueblos Agency records at NARA Denver cover all six Pueblos. The Jemez Mountains area includes Bandelier National Monument and the former Los Alamos Ranch School site. The town of Corrales and communities along the Rio Grande have Hispanic family records dating to Spanish colonial era through parish registers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Much land is Pueblo trust land. Pre-1903 in Bernalillo County.

source_profiles.source_name: Pueblo Mission & Sandoval County Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic mission records for Jemez, Zia, Santa Ana, Cochiti, San Felipe, Santo Domingo Pueblos. Among oldest church records in Western Hemisphere.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilmed; some gaps from Pueblo Revolt (1680)

source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Northern & Southern Pueblos
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Census rolls, allotment records, agency correspondence for six Pueblos in Sandoval County.
Marriage licenses from 1903.
Federal census from 1910. Pre-1903 under Bernalillo County. Includes Pueblo Indian schedules.
Spanish colonial land grants to Pueblos confirmed by US government. Each Pueblo holds a grant predating US sovereignty.

county_metadata.record_losses: No catastrophic losses; some Spanish colonial records deteriorated over centuries.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Santa Fe founded 1610 — oldest state capital in the US. NM State Records Center and Archives (SRCA) located here. Contains Tesuque, Pojoaque, and Nambe Pueblos.
county_metadata.research_tips: Santa Fe County is the epicenter of NM genealogical research. Santa Fe was founded in 1610, making it the oldest state capital, and the SRCA houses the Spanish Archives of New Mexico (SANM I & II), Mexican Archives (MANM), and territorial records — the core of NM colonial genealogy. The Cathedral Basilica of St. Francis of Assisi parish records date to the 1690s (post-Pueblo Revolt reconquest) and are on FamilySearch. The Palace of the Governors was the seat of government for Spain, Mexico, and the US territory. County Clerk has deeds from 1852. The NM History Museum and the Fray Angelico Chavez History Library hold manuscripts and genealogies. The Archdiocese of Santa Fe archives have church records spanning four centuries. Tesuque, Pojoaque, and Nambe Pueblo records supplement BIA records at NARA. The Court of Private Land Claims records at SRCA adjudicated land grants.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index; older records at SRCA

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial organization. Spanish and Mexican era land transfers at SRCA.
Catholic parish records from post-Pueblo Revolt reconquest. Cathedral Basilica, Our Lady of Guadalupe, and surrounding missions. Among oldest church records in the US.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilmed by FamilySearch; originals with Archdiocese

source_profiles.source_name: Santa Fe Land Grants & SANM Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish Archives of NM (SANM I — civil/military, SANM II — land grants), Mexican Archives (MANM), and Court of Private Land Claims records. The most comprehensive Spanish colonial archive in the Southwest.
Wills, estates, guardianships. Spanish-era testaments (testamentos) at SRCA.
Federal census 1850-1950. Colonial padrones and censuses at SRCA from 1750s.

source_profiles.source_name: Santa Fe New Mexican & Territorial Press
source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / SRCA / NM History Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Santa Fe New Mexican (founded 1849) is the oldest newspaper west of the Mississippi. Territorial government notices, land grant notices, obituaries.

source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Northern Pueblos Agency
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA census rolls and agency records for Pueblos in Santa Fe County.
